description: Kettle BH USA 2025 / DEFCON 33 'HTTP/1.1 must die' techniques: 0.CL (Content-Length: 0 + body), CL.0 (CL truthful to one server, ignored by next), V-H (visible Transfer-Encoding with semantic mismatch), Expect-100-based desync, RQP (request-queue poisoning), double-desync. Reference-only style for safety — operator drives the actual primer requests via send_raw_request + concurrent_requests; matchers below describe the expected anomaly per variant. Coordinate with test_request_smuggling for the executable harness.

zero_cl_desync.description:
0.CL — first server reads Content-Length: 0 and discards body; second server reads body as the next request. Detection: send POST with CL: 0 and a smuggled `GET /admin HTTP/1.1
Host: target

` body; follow-up request observes admin response or admin-only header.

probes.description: 0.CL primer. Reissue baseline request immediately afterwards — anomaly = baseline response served the smuggled GET

cl_zero_desync.description: CL.0 — frontend trusts CL; backend ignores it. Smuggled request body lives past the first server's read. Variant of 0.CL with reversed trust direction.

probes.description: CL.0 primer. Watch for unexpected /robots.txt response served on the next request

visible_te_desync.description: V-H (visible Transfer-Encoding) — one server processes TE chunked, the other reads CL. Differs from classic TE.CL by having the TE header in a form the second server *sees* but doesn't recognise (e.g. obsolete linear whitespace, multiple TE headers with same value).

probes.payload:
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Transfer-encoding : chunked
Content-Length: 6

0

XSSED
probes.description: Visible-TE primer. Smuggled `XSSED` appears as front of next request on backend

expect_100_desync.description: Expect: 100-continue based desync. Some intermediaries strip Expect after forwarding the headers; backend waits for 100-continue that never comes, eventually times out, then reads next request body as continuation.

probes.description: Expect 100-continue primer. Watch for follow-up request stalled or seeing SMUGGLED_BODY at start of its body

rqp_request_queue_poison.description: Request-Queue Poisoning — smuggled headers persist on a keep-alive connection that backend recycles across many fronted users. Single primer affects every subsequent request on that pooled connection.

probes.payload:
Transfer-Encoding: chunked
Connection: keep-alive

0

GET /healthz HTTP/1.1
X-Praetor-Poison: COLLABORATOR
probes.description: Persistent-poison primer. Follow with reissued benign request — vulnerable backend echoes X-Praetor-Poison or hits COLLABORATOR on the next user's traffic

double_desync_amplification.description: Double-desync — two primers in sequence achieve persistent state corruption that a single primer cannot. Useful when one of the desync conditions only fires after a particular header state is already wedged.

probes.payload:
Content-Length: 4
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

5c
GPOST / HTTP/1.1
Foo: bar

0
probes.description: Double-primer (CL+TE then nested malformed chunk). Anomaly = first response 200 with no body, second response with smuggled GPOST echoed
